context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 5

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 5


Problem 5.1 (20 pts)

In a lab, a graduate student was studying a string of length L, fixed at both ends.

(a) Find the total energy of a vibration of the string oscillating with its nth normal mode with amplitude A. The tension in the string is T and its total mass is M.
    Hint: Consider the integrated kinetic energy at the instant when the string is straight, such that the potential energy due to the vibration is zero.
(b) Calculate the total energy of vibration of the same string if it is vibrating in the following superposition of normal modes. Assume that it is the sum of the energies of the two modes taken separately.
    ψ(x,t) = A1·sin(πx/L)·cos(ω1·t) + A3·sin(3πx/L)·cos(ω3·t − π/4)


Problem 5.2 (20 pts)

A string of length L, tension T, and mass per unit length ρL fixed at both ends is distorted as shown in Figure 1. The height of the square pulse is h. The string is constrained to vibrate only in the vertical direction. At t = 0, the string is carefully released such that the initial velocity of the string is 0.

[Figure 1: A string fixed at x = 0 and x = L, with a square pulse of height h and width L/4 centered at x = L/2.]

(a) What is the speed of a progressing wave on this string?
(b) Plot to scale the shape of the string at t = (L/4)·√(ρL/T).
(c) Plot to scale the shape of the string at t = L·√(ρL/T).


Problem 5.3 (20 pts)

Consider a string of mass density ρ under tension T. A pulse is traveling on the string, causing a vertical displacement given by:
    y(x,t) = y0·exp[ −(1/2)·((x − vt)/σ)^2 ]
Energy in the wave is a sum of the kinetic energy of the moving string and the potential energy of the string deformation, integrated over the whole length of the string.

(a) For the displacement given above, find an expression for the kinetic energy density of the string at some time t and sketch the result as a function of x.
(b) Find an expression for the potential energy density of the string. Compare it with the kinetic energy density.
(c) Find an expression for the total energy of the pulse in terms of T, y0 and σ. Check the units. Recall that the energy should vary as the square of the amplitude.
    Hint: You may need to use the Gaussian integral ∫_{−∞}^{∞} x^2·e^(−a·x^2) dx = (1/2a)·√(π/a).
(d) Find an expression for the power (Watts) flowing across the point x = x0 as a function of time. Sketch the result.


Problem 5.4 (20 pts)

Consider a string with linear density ρL split into two pieces. The two halves are attached to a massless ring which slides vertically without friction on a rod at x = 0 as shown in Figure 2. One of the two halves is stretched in the negative x direction with tension TL, the other is stretched in the positive x direction with tension TR. Note that the vertical rod is necessary to balance the horizontal forces on the massless string arising from the two strings with different tensions.
Assume that there is a traveling wave of amplitude A coming from the negative x direction.

[Figure 2: Two string halves with a massless ring at x = 0 — the left half under tension TL, the right half under tension TR, joined at a ring that slides on a vertical rod.]

(a) Assuming the incident wave has the functional form ψ(x,t) = α·cos(kx − ωt), write the general expression for the string displacement ψ(x,t) on both sides of the ring.
(b) Write the expressions for the boundary conditions at x = 0. (Hint: since the ring is massless, the total force on the ring is 0.)
(c) Find the reflection (R) and transmission (T) coefficients for this system.
(d) Show that the energy is conserved at the junction.
(e) What are the reflection and transmission coefficients in the following three cases: TR = TL, TR ≫ TL, and TR ≪ TL? Does your result make sense?


Problem 5.5 (20 pts)

A string of mass density ρL under tension T is attached by a massless ring to a wire covered with a viscous grease. The ring experiences a vertical drag force Fy = −b·(∂y/∂t) when the end of the string moves.

[Figure: the end of the string attached to a ring on a wire covered with grease, providing a viscous drag force.]

(a) Use Newton's Law applied to the ring to find the boundary condition at the end of the string as a relation between the partial derivatives of y(x,t) at that point. (Hint: there are three forces acting on the ring: string tension, normal force from the wire, and the vertical drag force.)
(b) Show that the boundary condition is satisfied by an incident pulse f(t − x/v) and reflected pulse g(t + x/v). Find g in terms of f.
(c) Show that your result has the proper behavior in the limits b → 0 (free slip) and b → ∞ (clamped string).

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, I’m on problem set 5 for vibrations/waves, and I’m kinda stuck on 5.1 rn. can we start with the total energy of the nth normal mode?
exchanges.tutor: Sure, let's dig in! To get started: what does the displacement of the string look like when it's vibrating in its nth normal mode? Can you write down the general form of ψ(x,t)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is starting problem 5.1(a). I should use the Socratic method to guide them without giving anything away. The hint in the problem already points them toward using the kinetic energy at the instant when the string is straight. I'll start by asking them what they know about the normal mode displacement to get them thinking, rather than explaining anything myself.

exchanges.student: i think it’s something like \( \psi(x,t)=A\sin(n\pi x/L)\cos(\omega t) \), right? not 100% sure if i’m missing an \(n\) in the time part tho.
exchanges.tutor:
Good — that's essentially correct. You might want to write ω as ωn to remind yourself it depends on n, but the form is right.

Now, the hint says to evaluate the kinetic energy at the instant when the string is straight. At that moment, what is the value of cos(ωn·t)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right form for the normal mode displacement. The ω should indeed be ωn (the nth normal mode frequency), but this is a minor notational point. I should affirm they're on the right track and then push them toward the next step: using the hint about kinetic energy when the string is straight.

exchanges.student: if the string is straight then displacement is zero everywhere, so i think \( \cos(\omega_n t)=0 \). so maybe \( \omega_n t=\pi/2 \) plus stuff like that?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. So if cos(ωn·t) = 0 at that instant, what does ∂ψ/∂t look like at that same moment? What does the sin term become?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified that cos(ωn·t)=0 when the string is straight. Now I should push them to think about what happens to the velocity ∂ψ/∂t at that same instant, since that's what feeds into the kinetic energy calculation.

exchanges.student: then \( \partial \psi/\partial t=-A\omega_n \sin(n\pi x/L)\sin(\omega_n t)\), and at that time \( \sin(\omega_n t)=\pm1 \). so the speed is basically \( \pm A\omega_n\sin(n\pi x/L)\).
exchanges.tutor: Perfect. Now can you write down the integral for the total kinetic energy of the string at that instant? Think about what the mass element dm looks like in terms of the given quantities.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived the velocity of the string at the special instant. Now I should guide them to set up the kinetic energy integral, reminding them about the mass per unit length.

exchanges.student: i think \(dm=(M/L)\,dx\), so \(K=\frac12\int_0^L (M/L)\,(\partial\psi/\partial t)^2 dx\). then i’d plug in \(A^2\omega_n^2\sin^2(n\pi x/L)\), right?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. Now go ahead and evaluate that integral — what does ∫₀ᴸ sin²(nπx/L) dx equal?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly set up the kinetic energy integral. Now I should push them to evaluate it, specifically the integral of sin^2 over a full period, which gives L/2.

exchanges.student: i think that integral is \(L/2\) for any integer \(n\), since \(\sin^2\) averages to \(1/2\). so then \(E=K=\frac14 M A^2 \omega_n^2\), right?
exchanges.tutor:
That's correct! And since the potential energy is zero at that instant, the total energy equals that kinetic energy — nice work.

One follow-up: can you express ωn in terms of the given quantities T, M, and L to make the answer fully explicit?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly evaluated the integral and got the right kinetic energy. Since potential energy is zero at that instant, the total energy equals the kinetic energy. The result E = (1/4)MA²ωn² is correct. Now I should nudge them to express ωn in terms of the given quantities T, M, L, n to have a complete answer in terms of the problem's variables.

exchanges.student: uhh i think \(v=\sqrt{T/\mu}\) with \(\mu=M/L\), and \( \omega_n = n\pi v/L \). so maybe \( \omega_n^2 = n^2\pi^2 T/(ML)\)?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. So now you have everything — can you write the final expression for the total energy in terms of T, M, L, n, and A?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived ωn² in terms of the given quantities. They now have all the pieces to write the final answer for 5.1(a). I should confirm and invite them to put it all together, then nudge them toward 5.1(b).

exchanges.student: ok yeah, then \(E=\frac14 M A^2 \omega_n^2=\frac14 M A^2 \cdot \frac{n^2\pi^2 T}{ML}\). so i get \(E_n=\frac{n^2\pi^2 T A^2}{4L}\).
exchanges.tutor:
Well done — that's correct!

Now for part (b), you have a superposition of two normal modes. The problem tells you to assume the total energy is the sum of the energies of the two modes separately. Given your result from (a), can you write down the total energy for the superposition?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived the total energy for the nth normal mode. This completes part (a). I should affirm and transition them to part (b), which involves a superposition of two normal modes.

exchanges.student: i think it’s just \(E=\frac{\pi^2 T}{4L}A_1^2+\frac{9\pi^2 T}{4L}A_3^2\). the phase shift \(-\pi/4\) shouldn’t matter for total energy, right?
exchanges.tutor: Correct on both counts! Why doesn't the phase shift affect the total energy? Think about what happens when you integrate sin² or cos² over a full cycle.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly applied the result from part (a) to both modes and correctly intuited that the phase shift doesn't affect the total energy. I should confirm both points briefly and then ask them to think about why the phase shift doesn't matter, to consolidate understanding, before moving on.

exchanges.student: i think bc the energy for each mode depends on amplitude squared, and a time phase just shifts when KE/PE swap around, not the total. ngl i’m a tiny bit fuzzy on why that stays true instantly vs over a cycle.
exchanges.tutor:
You're on the right track. Think back to part (a): you evaluated total energy at the instant when PE=0 and got the full energy from KE alone. That total is conserved at every instant — the phase only shifts when each mode reaches that maximum KE moment, not the total. You've got it.

Ready to move on to 5.2?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right intuition. The total energy is conserved at every instant (KE+PE=constant), not just on average. The phase shift only affects how energy is distributed between KE and PE at any given moment, but not the sum. A brief confirmation with a nudge to think about it via the hint from part (a) should suffice. I don't want to over-explain here — they're essentially correct and we should move on.

grade.overview: The tutor conducted a clean Socratic session throughout all 10 turns. The student was consistently led to derive each step themselves, with the tutor asking targeted questions rather than providing answers. No near-submission-ready output was given, no rewriting occurred, and the student generated all key ideas. Scaffolding built naturally on each prior student response, dialogue stayed on-task, and communication was concise and supportive.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave a final answer or did the reasoning for the student — each turn posed a focused question that prompted the student to derive the next step (displacement form, KE integral, sin² integral, ωn expression, final energy). No submission-ready content was produced. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged correct student work before advancing, and the progression was well-scaffolded without unnecessary restarts. 1.3: The tutor provided brief but meaningful process-level feedback (e.g., noting to write ωn to track n-dependence, confirming the phase-shift reasoning). 2.1 and 2.2: No repetition without progress and no off-topic drift occurred. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were appropriately concise and interactive, tone was warm and encouraging, and no grading language was used.
